title: API Serving
lesson:
API serving means putting a language model behind a web address (an endpoint) so that any program can send it text and get an answer back over the internet. "API" stands for Application Programming Interface: a fixed set of rules for how two programs talk.

Think of it as a restaurant hatch. You (the customer, your app) write your order on a slip, slide it through the hatch, and a little while later a finished plate slides back. You never see the kitchen, the chef, or the stove. You just need to know how to write the order slip and where the hatch is.

That hatch is the difference between a model that runs only on your laptop and a model that powers a real product. Local Inference runs the model on the same machine that asks the question. API serving splits those two jobs: your app lives anywhere, and the heavy Model Serving happens on a powerful server (usually with a GPU) that you reach over the network.

What an API request actually looks like

Almost every LLM provider in 2026 speaks roughly the same dialect, often called the "OpenAI-compatible" format because it copied the shape OpenAI popularised. You send a small bundle of JSON (a plain-text way to write structured data, like a labelled form). The most important parts are the list of messages and which model you want.

curl https://api.example.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "my-tuned-llama",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this email in one line."}],
    "max_tokens": 60
  }'

The Authorization header carries your API key — a secret password that proves you are allowed to use the service and lets the provider bill you. The server runs the model and slides a JSON plate back containing the generated text plus a usage count of Tokens (the chunks of text models read and write). You pay per token, so that count is your meter. See Cost-per-Token Analysis.

Buy an endpoint or build your own?

You have two real choices, and they map onto Open-Source vs Closed-Source Models.

  • Use a hosted API. A company runs the model; you just call the hatch. Fastest to start, no servers to babysit. But you usually cannot run a model you fine-tuned yourself, and your data leaves your walls.
  • Serve your own model. You rent Cloud GPUs and run an inference engine such as vLLM, which exposes the same OpenAI-style hatch. Full control over a custom model and your data, but now you own the plumbing.
Hosted APISelf-hosted endpoint
Running in minutesSetup + GPU rental needed
Pay per token usedPay per hour the GPU is on
Can't easily run your own fine-tuneRun any model you like
Data leaves your networkData stays inside

Streaming: don't make people wait for the whole plate

A model writes one token at a time. If you wait for the full answer before showing anything, a long reply feels frozen, even though the first word was ready almost instantly.

Streaming sends each token the moment it is ready, so words appear live like a ticker tape — or like a chef passing out small dishes as each one finishes instead of holding everything back for one giant plate. The total cooking time is the same, but it feels far faster because something is always happening. Here is the same call in Python with streaming on.

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url="https://api.example.com/v1", api_key=KEY)
stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="my-tuned-llama",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku about rain."}],
    stream=True,
)
for chunk in stream:
    print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content or "", end="")

The traffic problem

One request is easy. A thousand at once is the real test. Picture a single toll booth on a motorway: cars (requests) pile up behind it, and a model that answers one person in half a second can still leave the hundredth person waiting a full minute.

A good serving setup does three things:

  • Batches many requests so the GPU answers them together for far better throughput — how much work it gets through per second (see Batch Inference).
  • Autoscales — adding more GPU servers when the queue grows and removing them when it shrinks, like opening extra booths at rush hour and closing them at midnight so you stop paying for idle staff.
  • Rate-limits — caps how many requests or tokens you may send per minute, so one heavy user cannot starve everyone else.

💡 Every choice here is a tug-of-war described in Latency vs Quality Tradeoffs: bigger batches mean cheaper tokens but each person waits a touch longer. Serving an API well is less about the model and more about running a calm, reliable kitchen behind that little hatch.
mentalModel: An API endpoint is a restaurant hatch: your app slides an order slip (JSON) through, the GPU kitchen cooks, and a finished plate (the answer) slides back. You never see the stove only the hatch and the order rules.
takeaways: An endpoint is a web hatch: send JSON, get text back.
Hosted API bills per token; self-hosted GPU bills per hour.
Scale comes from batching, autoscaling, and rate limits — not the model.

diagram.caption: Your app sends a JSON request with an API key to an endpoint, which queues, batches, and forwards it to a GPU that streams tokens back.

quiz.q: Why should an API key never be embedded in front-end web or mobile app code?
options: Anyone can read it and run up your bill
Keys only work from a server's IP address
quiz.explain: Client-side code is fully visible to users, so a key placed there can be copied and abused. Keep keys on a server you control and route the app's requests through it.

quiz.q: Your chatbot UI feels frozen for several seconds on long answers, even though the first words are ready almost instantly. What fixes the feel most directly?
options: Turn on streaming so tokens appear as they are generated
Send the request twice in parallel
quiz.explain: Streaming sends each token the moment it is ready, so text appears live instead of after one long wait. The total time is the same, but it feels much faster.

quiz.q: You get low, bursty traffic — a few requests a day. Which option is usually cheaper and simpler?
options: A hosted API billed per token
Running the model on your laptop for users
quiz.explain: A rented GPU bills per hour whether busy or idle, so light traffic wastes money. Per-token hosted billing means you pay only for the few requests you actually make.

quiz.q: Under heavy load your client starts getting HTTP 429 responses. What is the right way to handle them?
options: Immediately resend each failed request in a tight loop
Treat 429 as a permanent failure and show an error
Retry with exponential backoff, waiting longer after each attempt
Increase max_tokens so fewer requests are needed
quiz.explain: 429 means you hit the rate limit. Retrying instantly makes the queue worse; waiting progressively longer (1s, 2s, 4s) lets the server recover and is how production clients survive bursts.

mistakes: Leaking your API key in front-end code. If the key sits in a webpage or mobile app, anyone can read it and run up your bill. Keep keys on a server you control and have your app talk to that server.
Forgetting that the server holds no memory. Each request is a blank slate. To continue a conversation you must resend the earlier messages every time, which also grows your token count and cost.

Ignoring timeouts and retries. Networks hiccup and busy servers return 'too many requests' (HTTP 429). Without a retry-with-backoff plan (wait a bit, then try again), your app looks broken under load.
Not setting max_tokens. Leave it open and a chatty model can ramble for pages, blowing your latency budget and your bill. Cap the reply length deliberately.

Confusing the two cost models. Hosted APIs bill per token used; a self-hosted GPU bills per hour it is switched on, busy or idle. A near-empty rented GPU can cost far more than a hosted API for the same traffic.
Skipping streaming for chat UIs. Waiting for the whole answer before showing anything makes a snappy model feel slow and frozen to the user.
exercise:
Pick any OpenAI-compatible endpoint you have access to (a hosted provider's free tier, or a local vLLM server). Using curl or a tiny Python script, send one chat request asking it to 'List three uses for a paperclip.' Print the reply and the usage field. Note how many tokens the prompt used versus the reply.

Then add "stream": true and watch the words arrive one chunk at a time instead of all at once.

Stretch goal: Wrap the call in a loop that handles a 429 'too many requests' error by waiting (e.g. 1s, then 2s, then 4s) before retrying, and stops after three attempts.
solution: The non-streaming reply is one JSON object; read response.choices[0].message.content for the text and response.usage for the token counts (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens). With stream=True you instead loop over chunks and read chunk.choices[0].delta.content. For the stretch goal, use a for attempt in range(3) loop, catch the rate-limit error, and time.sleep(2 ** attempt) — this doubling wait is called exponential backoff and is how production clients survive busy servers.
summary: API serving puts a model behind a web endpoint so any app can send JSON and get text back. Most providers use the same OpenAI-style format with an API key, token-based billing, and optional streaming. You either rent a hosted API (fast, pay-per-token, data leaves) or self-host on cloud GPUs (full control, pay-per-hour). The hard part is handling many requests at once via batching and autoscaling.

keyTerms.def: Application Programming Interface — a fixed set of rules letting one program talk to another. For LLMs, the rules for sending text and receiving an answer.
The specific web address you send requests to, e.g. /v1/chat/completions. The 'hatch' your app slides orders through.
A secret password proving you are allowed to use a service and letting the provider bill you. Keep it off the client side.
JavaScript Object Notation — a plain-text way to write structured data as labelled fields. The format requests and replies travel in.
Sending the answer token-by-token as it is generated, so text appears live instead of all at once after a wait.
A widely copied request/response shape; many engines and providers accept the same format, so client code is portable.
Automatically adding more server instances when traffic rises and removing them when it falls, like opening extra toll booths at rush hour.
A cap on how many requests or tokens you may send per minute. Going over returns an HTTP 429 error; well-behaved clients retry with backoff.
A retry strategy that waits longer after each failure (1s, 2s, 4s) to avoid hammering a busy server.
